text: The surgeon begins the colectomy procedure with a medial to lateral approach, a common technique for this type of surgery.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection in the abdominal cavity, likely targeting the affected area in the left lower quadrant.
The surgeon approaches the superior hemorrhoidal artery, using a ligature device to navigate the inflamed tissues.
The surgeon elevates the superior hemorrhoidal vessel and enters the retro-rectal space, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and proceeds to ligate the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, addressing the challenges posed by the phlegmon on the opposite side during the medial dissection.
The surgeon addresses the phlegmon attached to the left dome of the bladder and the anterior abdominal wall, noting its significant adherence to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to carefully separate the inflamed tissue around the phlegmon attached to the left dome of the bladder.
The surgeon attempts to enter a natural plane more cephalad, moving deeper into the retroperitoneum than intended, and identifies the correct plane at a specific location.
The surgeon attempts to re-enter the retroperitoneal plane by dissecting at the current level, carefully separating the white line from the retroperitoneum and mesocolon.
The surgeon switches the camera to ICG mode, aiming to visualize the left ureter with indocyanine green fluorescence, which should appear green if the ureter is approached closely.
The surgeon enters a chronic perforated cavity located just beneath the dome of the bladder, allowing for safer dissection by clearly identifying anatomical structures within the cavity.
The surgeon observes the left adnexa being drawn into the disease process, requiring careful negotiation. The round ligament on the left side is visibly involved in the dissection area.
The surgeon performs a left salpingectomy and oophorectomy en bloc with the specimen, visualizing the left fallopian tube and ovary being removed together.
The surgeon visualizes the left ureter, which appears green due to the ICG fluorescence, facilitating the separation of the phlegmon without concern.
The surgeon safely dissects and removes attachments from the distal sigmoid colon and upper rectum, freeing them from the lateral sidewall.

The surgeon takes the proximal mesentery intracorporeally to facilitate a fan and steel extraction, ensuring no tearing occurs. The mesentery reaches adequately, and the surgeon proceeds to take the distal mesentery.
The surgeon navigates through the inflamed mesentery of the upper rectum, which is visibly affected by previous perforation and chronic adherence to the surrounding cavity.
The surgeon attempts to achieve hemostasis in the surgical field, likely using techniques or instruments to control bleeding.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to confirm the blood supply to the proximal and distal margins, which appears adequate. The distal segment is then divided in preparation for extracorporeal extraction of the specimen and anvil placement at the planned Pfannenstiel incision site.
The surgeon divides the distal section of the colon, preparing for extraction through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision. An anvil is placed for the upcoming anastomosis.

The surgeon prepares for EEA anastomosis, using laparoscopic guidance to navigate the stapler through intestinal folds and ensure the spike reaches the apex. The anvil is attached, checking that the mesentery is not twisted. The Baker anastomosis is performed as a side-to-end connection.
The surgeon inspects the mesentery for twisting and tension, applying compression before withdrawing the stapler. The anastomosis is tested for leaks, which shows no signs of leakage.
